title: SLURM Training Jobs on HPC Clusters
subtitle: An HPC training run is a resource request, a batch script, an environment, and a reproducibility contract.
summary: The SLM research project used Rutgers Amarel HPC and SLURM batch scripts to distribute training and evaluation jobs.

artifact.title: Job eligibility under resource constraints
artifact.description: Change GPUs and wall time to see which jobs can fit a scheduler request.

contentBlocks.content:
A minimal `sbatch` script has three layers:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=slm-sft
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:2
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --mem=64G

module load cuda
python train.py --config configs/gsm8k-sft.yml
```

The scheduler reads the directives, queues the job, and runs it when resources are available.

sections.heading: What the Scheduler Decides
body: SLURM does not just run code. It decides when a job can run based on requested resources, partitions, limits, priority, and cluster availability.
A request for too much time or too many GPUs can sit in the queue longer. A request for too little can fail mid-training.

sections.heading: Reproducibility for Training
body: A good training job records code commit, config file, dataset version, environment, seed, hardware, and output checkpoint path.
Without those details, a result like 'GSM8k improved' is not reproducible. The cluster job becomes a one-off event rather than a scientific artifact.

pitfalls: Requesting resources without measuring actual usage.
Training from a dirty or unrecorded code state.
Writing checkpoints to temporary storage accidentally.
Ignoring failed or preempted jobs in results.

quiz.question: What does sbatch do?
options: Submits a batch script to Slurm
quiz.explanation: The Slurm sbatch command submits batch scripts to the scheduler.
